question.text: Lease liability on a balance sheet arises when:

answer.text: A company owns an asset outright
A company acquires the right to use an asset under a lease agreement for more than one year
A company writes off bad debts

explanation.text: **The Logic:** When a company leases an asset for more than one year, it recognizes a lease liability representing the fair value of lease minus amounts repaid. Analysts often treat this as part of debt. **The Trap:** Ownership means PPE (not lease). Selling creates a gain/loss. Bad debts are provisions, not lease liabilities. **Cross-Exam Context:** Lease accounting tested in NISM XV Ch 8. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** IndAS 116 requires most leases to be on-balance-sheet. This increased reported debt for companies with significant operating leases (retail, aviation).
